text:
National Sock Confusion Day falls on February 17th every year. No one remembers who started it. The story goes that a statistician in Cleveland noticed that exactly 73% of people own at least one sock without a partner, and he filed paperwork with a made-up federal agency to make it official. The paperwork was approved because no one checked.

Here is how you celebrate.

At exactly 7am you must open your sock drawer and deliberately mismatch every pair you own. This is not optional. Polka dots with stripes. Holiday-themed socks in February. One ankle sock, one knee-high. Participants take this seriously.

The greeting for the day is "Where is the other one?" You say this to coworkers, strangers, your mail carrier. They are expected to shrug. Shrugging is the correct response. Nodding is considered rude.

Many offices hold Sock Tribunals, where employees bring their most mysteriously disappeared socks and present theories about where the match went. Common theories include: the dryer ate it, a roommate took it, it achieved sentience and left on principle. A panel of three judges awards points for creativity. There are no prizes. The winner gets to shrug the longest.

At lunch, restaurants that participate serve everything in mismatched dishes. Your soup comes in a mug. Your coffee comes in a bowl. Nobody complains because that is also part of it.

Children in schools spend the afternoon constructing sock puppets using only orphaned socks. The puppet must represent their theory about where missing socks go. The most popular answer, year after year, is simply "elsewhere."

At midnight you are supposed to check your dryer one more time. You will find a sock you have never seen before. It does not match anything you own. You keep it anyway.

This is the whole point of the holiday. Nobody knows what the point is. That is also the point.

Happy Sock Confusion Day.
